## DEFORMABLE DETR: DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS - FOR END TO-END OBJECT DETECTION
**Xizhou Zhu**[1] _[∗]_ **, Weijie Su**[2] _[∗‡]_ **, Lewei Lu**[1] **, Bin Li**[2] **, Xiaogang Wang**[1] _[,]_[3] **, Jifeng Dai**[1] _[†]_

1SenseTime Research

2University of Science and Technology of China

3The Chinese University of Hong Kong
_{_ zhuwalter,luotto,daijifeng _}_ @sensetime.com jackroos@mail.ustc.edu.cn, binli@ustc.edu.cn xgwang@ee.cuhk.edu.hk

## ABSTRACT

DETR has been recently proposed to eliminate the need for many hand-designed components in object detection while demonstrating good performance. However, it suffers from slow convergence and limited feature spatial resolution, due to the limitation of Transformer attention modules in processing image feature maps. To mitigate these issues, we proposed Deformable DETR, whose attention modules only attend to a small set of key sampling points around a reference. Deformable DETR can achieve better performance than DETR (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Extensive experiments on the COCO benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Code is released at https:// github.com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Modern object detectors employ many hand-crafted components (Liu et al., 2020), e.g., anchor generation, rule-based training target assignment, non-maximum suppression (NMS) post-processing. They are not fully end-to-end. Recently, Carion et al. (2020) proposed DETR to eliminate the need for such hand-crafted components, and built the first fully end-to-end object detector, achieving very competitive performance. DETR utilizes a simple architecture, by combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) encoder-decoders. They exploit the versatile and powerful relation modeling capability of Transformers to replace the hand-crafted rules, under properly designed training signals.

Despite its interesting design and good performance, DETR has its own issues: (1) It requires much longer training epochs to converge than the existing object detectors. For example, on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark, DETR needs 500 epochs to converge, which is around 10 to 20 times slower than Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015). (2) DETR delivers relatively low performance at detecting small objects. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features, where small objects are detected from high-resolution feature maps. Meanwhile, high-resolution feature maps lead to unacceptable complexities for DETR. The above-mentioned issues can be mainly attributed to the deficit of Transformer components in processing image feature maps. At initialization, the attention modules cast nearly uniform attention weights to all the pixels in the feature maps. Long training epoches is necessary for the attention weights to be learned to focus on sparse meaningful locations. On the other hand, the attention weights computation in Transformer encoder is of quadratic computation w.r.t. pixel numbers. Thus, it is of very high computational and memory complexities to process high-resolution feature maps.

In the image domain, deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017) is of a powerful and efficient mechanism to attend to sparse spatial locations. It naturally avoids the above-mentioned issues. While it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism, which is the key for the success of DETR.

In this paper, we propose _Deformable DETR_ , which mitigates the slow convergence and high complexity issues of DETR. It combines the best of the sparse spatial sampling of deformable convolution, and the relation modeling capability of Transformers. We propose the _deformable attention module_ , which attends to a small set of sampling locations as a pre-filter for prominent key elements out of all the feature map pixels. The module can be naturally extended to aggregating multi-scale features, without the help of FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). In Deformable DETR , we utilize (multi-scale) deformable attention modules to replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps, as shown in Fig. 1.

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. We explore a simple and effective _iterative bounding box refinement_ mechanism to improve the detection performance. We also try a _two-stage Deformable DETR_ , where the region proposals are also generated by a vaiant of Deformable DETR, which are further fed into the decoder for iterative bounding box refinement.

Extensive experiments on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Compared with DETR, Deformable DETR can achieve better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. The proposed variant of two-stage Deformable DETR can further improve the performance. Code is released at https://github. com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Efficient Attention Mechanism.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) involve both self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms. One of the most well-known concern of Transformers is the high time and memory complexity at vast key element numbers, which hinders model scalability in many cases. Recently, many efforts have been made to address this problem (Tay et al., 2020b), which can be roughly divided into three categories in practice.

The first category is to use pre-defined sparse attention patterns on keys. The most straightforward paradigm is restricting the attention pattern to be fixed local windows. Most works (Liu et al., 2018a; Parmar et al., 2018; Child et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Ho et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a; Hu et al., 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020; Ainslie et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020) follow this paradigm. Although restricting the attention pattern to a local neighborhood can decrease the complexity, it loses global information. To compensate, Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a) attend key elements

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

at fixed intervals to significantly increase the receptive field on keys. Beltagy et al. (2020); Ainslie et al. (2020); Zaheer et al. (2020) allow a small number of special tokens having access to all key elements. Zaheer et al. (2020); Qiu et al. (2019) also add some pre-fixed sparse attention patterns to attend distant key elements directly.

The second category is to learn data-dependent sparse attention. Kitaev et al. (2020) proposes a locality sensitive hashing (LSH) based attention, which hashes both the query and key elements to different bins. A similar idea is proposed by Roy et al. (2020), where k-means finds out the most related keys. Tay et al. (2020a) learns block permutation for block-wise sparse attention.

The third category is to explore the low-rank property in self-attention. Wang et al. (2020b) reduces the number of key elements through a linear projection on the size dimension instead of the channel dimension. Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) rewrite the calculation of selfattention through kernelization approximation.

In the image domain, the designs of efficient attention mechanism (e.g., Parmar et al. (2018); Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a); Hu et al. (2019); Ramachandran et al. (2019)) are still limited to the first category. Despite the theoretically reduced complexity, Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019) admit such approaches are much slower in implementation than traditional convolution with the same FLOPs (at least 3 _×_ slower), due to the intrinsic limitation in memory access patterns.

On the other hand, as discussed in Zhu et al. (2019a), there are variants of convolution, such as deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b) and dynamic convolution (Wu et al., 2019), that also can be viewed as self-attention mechanisms. Especially, deformable convolution operates much more effectively and efficiently on image recognition than Transformer self-attention. Meanwhile, it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism.

Our proposed deformable attention module is inspired by deformable convolution, and belongs to the second category. It only focuses on a small fixed set of sampling points predicted from the feature of query elements. Different from Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019), deformable attention is just slightly slower than the traditional convolution under the same FLOPs.

**Multi-scale Feature Representation for Object Detection.** One of the main difficulties in object detection is to effectively represent objects at vastly different scales. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features to accommodate this. As one of the pioneering works, FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) proposes a top-down path to combine multi-scale features. PANet (Liu et al., 2018b) further adds an bottom-up path on the top of FPN. Kong et al. (2018) combines features from all scales by a global attention operation. Zhao et al. (2019) proposes a U-shape module to fuse multi-scale features. Recently, NAS-FPN (Ghiasi et al., 2019) and Auto-FPN (Xu et al., 2019) are proposed to automatically design cross-scale connections via neural architecture search. Tan et al. (2020) proposes the BiFPN, which is a repeated simplified version of PANet. Our proposed multi-scale deformable attention module can naturally aggregate multi-scale feature maps via attention mechanism, without the help of these feature pyramid networks.

## 3 REVISITING TRANSFORMERS AND DETR

**Multi-Head Attention in Transformers.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) are of a network architecture based on attention mechanisms for machine translation. Given a query element (e.g., a target word in the output sentence) and a set of key elements (e.g., source words in the input sentence), the _multi-head attention module_ adaptively aggregates the key contents according to the attention weights that measure the compatibility of query-key pairs. To allow the model focusing on contents from different representation subspaces and different positions, the outputs of different attention heads are linearly aggregated with learnable weights. There are two known issues with Transformers. One is Transformers need long training schedules before convergence. Suppose the number of query and key elements are of _Nq_ and _Nk_ , respectively. Typically, with proper parameter initialization, _**U** m_ _**z** q_ and _**V** m_ _**x** k_ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1, which makes attention weights _Amqk ≈ Nk_[,][when] _[N][k]_[is][large.][It][will][lead] to ambiguous gradients for input features. Thus, long training schedules are required so that the attention weights can focus on specific keys. In the image domain, where the key elements are usually of image pixels, _Nk_ can be very large and the convergence is tedious.

On the other hand, the computational and memory complexity for multi-head attention can be very high with numerous query and key elements. The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of _O_ ( _NqC_[2] + _NkC_[2] + _NqNkC_ ). In the image domain, where the query and key elements are both of pixels, _Nq_ = _Nk ≫ C_ , the complexity is dominated by the third term, as _O_ ( _NqNkC_ ). Thus, the multi-head attention module suffers from a quadratic complexity growth with the feature map size.

**DETR.** DETR (Carion et al., 2020) is built upon the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture, combined with a set-based Hungarian loss that forces unique predictions for each ground-truth bounding box via bipartite matching. We briefly review the network architecture as follows.

Given the input feature maps _**x** ∈_ R _[C][×][H][×][W]_ extracted by a CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet (He et al., 2016)), DETR exploits a standard Transformer encoder-decoder architecture to transform the input feature maps to be features of a set of object queries. A 3-layer feed-forward neural network (FFN) and a linear projection are added on top of the object query features (produced by the decoder) as the detection head. The FFN acts as the regression branch to predict the bounding box coordinates _**b** ∈_ [0 _,_ 1][4] , where _**b**_ = _{bx, by, bw, bh}_ encodes the normalized box center coordinates, box height and width (relative to the image size). The linear projection acts as the classification branch to produce the classification results.

For the Transformer encoder in DETR, both query and key elements are of pixels in the feature maps. The inputs are of ResNet feature maps (with encoded positional embeddings). Let _H_ and _W_ denote the feature map height and width, respectively. The computational complexity of self-attention is of _O_ ( _H_[2] _W_[2] _C_ ), which grows quadratically with the spatial size.

For the Transformer decoder in DETR, the input includes both feature maps from the encoder, and _N_ object queries represented by learnable positional embeddings (e.g., _N_ = 100). There are two types of attention modules in the decoder, namely, cross-attention and self-attention modules. In the cross-attention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps. The query elements are of the object queries, and key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In it, _Nq_ = _N_ , _Nk_ = _H × W_ and the complexity of the cross-attention is of _O_ ( _HWC_[2] + _NHWC_ ). The complexity grows linearly with the spatial size of feature maps. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, so as to capture their relations. The query and key elements are both of the object queries. In it, _Nq_ = _Nk_ = _N_ , and the complexity of the self-attention module is of _O_ (2 _NC_[2] + _N_[2] _C_ ). The complexity is acceptable with moderate number of object queries.

DETR is an attractive design for object detection, which removes the need for many hand-designed components. However, it also has its own issues. These issues can be mainly attributed to the deficits of Transformer attention in handling image feature maps as key elements: (1) DETR has relatively low performance in detecting small objects. Modern object detectors use high-resolution feature maps to better detect small objects. However, high-resolution feature maps would lead to an unacceptable complexity for the self-attention module in the Transformer encoder of DETR, which has a quadratic complexity with the spatial size of input feature maps. (2) Compared with modern object detectors, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge. This is mainly because the attention modules processing image features are difficult to train. For example, at initialization, the cross-attention modules are almost of average attention on the whole feature maps. While, at the end of the training, the attention maps are learned to be very sparse, focusing only on the object

## 4 METHOD

## 4.1 DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS FOR END-TO-END OBJECT DETECTION

**Deformable Attention Module.** The core issue of applying Transformer attention on image feature maps is that it would look over all possible spatial locations. To address this, we present a _deformable attention module_ . Inspired by deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b), the deformable attention module only attends to a small set of key sampling points around a reference point, regardless of the spatial size of the feature maps, as shown in Fig. 2. By assigning only a small fixed number of keys for each query, the issues of convergence and feature spatial resolution can be mitigated.

**Deformable Transformer Encoder.** We replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps in DETR with the proposed multi-scale deformable attention module. Both the input and output of the encoder are of multi-scale feature maps with the same resolutions. In encoder, we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet (He et al., 2016) (transformed by a 1 _×_ 1 convolution), where _Cl_ is of resolution 2 _[l]_ lower than the input image. The lowest resolution feature map _**x**[L]_ is obtained via a 3 _×_ 3 stride 2 convolution on the final _C_ 5 stage, denoted as _C_ 6. All the multi-scale feature maps are of _C_ = 256 channels. Note that the top-down structure in FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) is not used, because our proposed multi-scale deformable attention in itself can exchange information among multi-scale feature maps. The constructing of multi-scale feature maps are also illustrated in Appendix A.2. Experiments in Section 5.2 show that adding FPN will not improve the performance. In application of the multi-scale deformable attention module in encoder, the output are of multiscale feature maps with the same resolutions as the input. Both the key and query elements are of pixels from the multi-scale feature maps. For each query pixel, the reference point is itself. To identify which feature level each query pixel lies in, we add a scale-level embedding, denoted as _**e** l_ , to the feature representation, in addition to the positional embedding. Different from the positional embedding with fixed encodings, the scale-level embedding _{_ _**e** l}[L] l_ =1[are][randomly][initialized][and] jointly trained with the network.

**Deformable Transformer Decoder.** There are cross-attention and self-attention modules in the decoder. The query elements for both types of attention modules are of object queries. In the crossattention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps, where the key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, where the key elements are of the object queries. Since our proposed deformable attention module is designed for processing convolutional feature maps as key elements, we only replace each cross-attention module to be the multi-scale deformable attention module, while leaving the self-attention modules unchanged. For each object query, the 2-d normalized coordinate of the

reference point _**p**_ ˆ _q_ is predicted from its object query embedding via a learnable linear projection followed by a sigmoid function.

Because the multi-scale deformable attention module extracts image features around the reference point, we let the detection head predict the bounding box as relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point to further reduce the optimization difficulty. The reference point is used as the initial guess of the box center. The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point. Check Appendix A.3 for the details. In this way, the learned decoder attention will have strong correlation with the predicted bounding boxes, which also accelerates the training convergence.
By replacing Transformer attention modules with deformable attention modules in DETR, we establish an efficient and fast converging detection system, dubbed as Deformable DETR (see Fig. 1).

## 4.2 ADDITIONAL IMPROVEMENTS AND VARIANTS FOR DEFORMABLE DETR

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit various variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. Due to limited space, we only introduce the core ideas of these improvements and variants here. The implementation details are given in Appendix A.4.

**Iterative Bounding Box Refinement.** This is inspired by the iterative refinement developed in optical flow estimation (Teed & Deng, 2020). We establish a simple and effective iterative bounding box refinement mechanism to improve detection performance. Here, each decoder layer refines the bounding boxes based on the predictions from the previous layer.

**Two-Stage Deformable DETR.** In the original DETR, object queries in the decoder are irrelevant to the current image. Inspired by two-stage object detectors, we explore a variant of Deformable DETR for generating region proposals as the first stage. The generated region proposals will be fed into the decoder as object queries for further refinement, forming a two-stage Deformable DETR.

In the first stage, to achieve high-recall proposals, each pixel in the multi-scale feature maps would serve as an object query. However, directly setting object queries as pixels will bring unacceptable computational and memory cost for the self-attention modules in the decoder, whose complexity grows quadratically with the number of queries. To avoid this problem, we remove the decoder and form an encoder-only Deformable DETR for region proposal generation. In it, each pixel is assigned as an object query, which directly predicts a bounding box. Top scoring bounding boxes are picked as region proposals. No NMS is applied before feeding the region proposals to the second stage.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

**Dataset.** We conduct experiments on COCO 2017 dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Our models are trained on the train set, and evaluated on the val set and test-dev set.

**Implementation Details.** ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) pre-trained ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) is utilized as the backbone for ablations. Multi-scale feature maps are extracted without FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). _M_ = 8 and _K_ = 4 are set for deformable attentions by default. Parameters of the deformable Transformer encoder are shared among different feature levels. Other hyper-parameter setting and training strategy mainly follow DETR (Carion et al., 2020), except that Focal Loss (Lin et al., 2017b) with loss weight of 2 is used for bounding box classification, and the number of object queries is increased from 100 to 300. We also report the performance of DETR-DC5 with these modifications for a fair comparison, denoted as DETR-DC5[+] . By default, models are trained for 50 epochs and the learning rate is decayed at the 40-th epoch by a factor of 0.1. Following DETR(Carion et al., 2020), we train our models using Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with base learning rate of 2 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] , _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, and weight decay of 10 _[−]_[4] . Learning rates of the linear projections, used for predicting object query reference points and sampling offsets, are multiplied by a factor of 0.1. Run time is evaluated on NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU.

## 5.1 COMPARISON WITH DETR

As shown in Table 1, compared with Faster R-CNN + FPN, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge, and delivers lower performance at detecting small objects. Compared with

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

DETR, Deformable DETR achieves better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Detailed convergence curves are shown in Fig. 3. With the aid of iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage paradigm, our method can further improve the detection accuracy.

Our proposed Deformable DETR has on par FLOPs with Faster R-CNN + FPN and DETR-DC5. But the runtime speed is much faster (1.6 _×_ ) than DETR-DC5, and is just 25% slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN. The speed issue of DETR-DC5 is mainly due to the large amount of memory access in Transformer attention. Our proposed deformable attention can mitigate this issue, at the cost of unordered memory access. Thus, it is still slightly slower than traditional convolution.

## 5.2 ABLATION STUDY ON DEFORMABLE ATTENTION

Table 2 presents ablations for various design choices of the proposed deformable attention module. Using multi-scale inputs instead of single-scale inputs can effectively improve detection accuracy with 1 _._ 7% AP, especially on small objects with 2 _._ 9% APS. Increasing the number of sampling points _K_ can further improve 0 _._ 9% AP. Using multi-scale deformable attention, which allows information exchange among different scale levels, can bring additional 1 _._ 5% improvement in AP. Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance. When multi-scale attention is not applied, and _K_ = 1, our (multi-scale) deformable attention module degenerates to deformable convolution, delivering noticeable lower accuracy.

## 5.3 COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS

Table 3 compares the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods. Iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage mechanism are both utilized by our models in Table 3. With ResNet-101 and ResNeXt-101 (Xie et al., 2017), our method achieves 48.7 AP and 49.0 AP without bells and whistles, respectively. By using ResNeXt-101 with DCN (Zhu et al., 2019b), the accuracy rises to 50.1 AP. With additional test-time augmentations, the proposed method achieves 52.3 AP.

## 6 CONCLUSION

Deformable DETR is an end-to-end object detector, which is efficient and fast-converging. It enables us to explore more interesting and practical variants of end-to-end object detectors. At the core of Deformable DETR are the (multi-scale) deformable attention modules, which is an efficient attention mechanism in processing image feature maps. We hope our work opens up new possibilities in exploring end-to-end object detection.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a high-quality and timely contribution to the field of computer vision by addressing the two primary bottlenecks of the DETR architecture: prohibitive training duration and poor performance on high-resolution (small object) features. The clarity of the writing is excellent; the authors define a clear technical gap—the quadratic complexity of global self-attention—and propose a logically sound remedy by combining the spatial sparsity of deformable convolutions with the relational modeling of Transformers. The introduction of "Deformable Attention" is a significant structural innovation that elegantly bypasses the need for traditional Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) by natively aggregating multi-scale features. While the conceptual framework is robust, the most significant issue for a paper of this magnitude is the risk of increased hyperparameter sensitivity, specifically regarding the number of sampling points ($K$) and the reference point initialization, which could affect the robustness of the "fast convergence" claim. Overall, this work represents a major efficiency milestone for end-to-end detection, backed by impressive empirical gains on the COCO benchmark.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in attention weight distribution claim
comments.quote: Typically, with proper parameter initialization, $**U** m$ $**z** q$ and $**V** m$ $**x** k$ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1, which makes attention weights $Amqk \approx Nk$[,][when] $[N][k]_[is][large.]
comments.explanation: The authors discuss why Transformers converge slowly due to uniform attention weights at initialization. However, there are two significant numerical issues. First, the text says the mean of projected features is '1 0', which is likely an OCR error for '0' (standard Xavier/Kaiming initialization). Second, it states $A_{mqk} \approx N_k$ when $N_k$ is large. In a softmax distribution where weights must sum to 1, a uniform distribution results in $A_{mqk} \approx 1/N_k$. Stating the weight is approximately $N_k$ is a mathematical impossibility for normalized attention and contradicts the logic that weights are small and gradients are ambiguous.

comments.title: Inconsistent Complexity Calculation and Scaling Claims

However, the formula $O(2N_qC^2 + \min(HWC^2, N_qKC^2))$ includes an $HWC^2$ term. This suggests the cost of the initial linear projection for Value embeddings is still dependent on the full feature map size. Furthermore, the $N_qKC^2$ term implies projection happens for every sampled point per query, whereas the linear scaling claim relies on $K$ being a small constant. The phrasing 'irrelevant to spatial size' is misleading if the entry-level $1 \times 1$ convolutions ($HWC^2$) are required for the module to function.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'relation modeling' vs. 'deformable convolution'

comments.explanation: The authors argue that deformable convolution lacks 'element relation modeling', necessitating Deformable Transformers. However, they later show the module degenerates to deformable convolution under certain settings. Since modern variants (DCNv2) include data-dependent modulation scalars, the distinction between 'relation modeling' (attention weights) and 'dynamic weighting' (convolutional modulation) is thin. The paper doesn't sufficiently clarify why the proposed mechanism constitutes 'relation modeling' while a multi-head modulated deformable convolution would not.

comments.title: Missing Multi-Head Indexing and Slicing in Equation 2

If they are in pixels and not re-scaled relative to the resolution, the 'receptive field' of the offsets would change inconsistently across different scales. The lack of clarity on the units of $\Delta p$ makes the multi-scale aggregation logic difficult to reproduce accurately.

comments.title: Possible misclassification of proposed method in related work categories

comments.explanation: The authors categorize attention into (1) pre-defined sparse, (2) data-dependent sparse, and (3) low-rank. They place Deformable DETR in (2) alongside LSH-based Reformers. However, Deformable DETR uses a fixed number of sampling points $K$ and local offsets, which structurally resembles category (1) local windows, but with learnable movement. The examples provided for category (2) (global similarity search like k-means) make the classification of their local sampling approach slightly ambiguous.

comments.title: Possible misclassification of proposed method in related work categories

comments.explanation: The authors categorize attention mechanisms into three groups: (1) pre-defined sparse patterns, (2) data-dependent sparse attention, and (3) low-rank approximations. They place Deformable DETR in the second category. However, in the second category, they list LSH-based attention (Reformers) and k-means clustering. While Deformable DETR's sampling locations are indeed data-dependent (predicted from query features), the number of sampled keys $K$ is fixed and the sparsity pattern is local/deformable rather than a global search for similarity like LSH or k-means. A careful reader might argue it shares significant DNA with the first category (local windows) but with learnable offsets. While not a technical error, the classification is slightly ambiguous given the examples provided for each category.

comments.title: Potentially misleading complexity claim regarding cross-attention

While the core attention calculation is indeed independent of $HW$, the authors mention in Section 4.1 that the sampling offsets and weights are obtained via linear projection over the query feature $z_q$, and the values are sampled using bilinear interpolation. However, in most implementations and the provided context of Figure 2, the 'Input Feature Map $x$' still needs to be processed (e.g., via a linear projection $W'_m x$ to create the Value embeddings). If this linear projection is applied to the full feature map $HW$ before sampling, there is still a term $O(HWC)$ or $O(HWC^2)$ present in the overall module complexity. The authors do mention a complexity of $O(2N_qC^2 + \min(HWC^2, N_qKC^2))$ earlier, but the phrasing 'irrelevant to the spatial size' in the decoder context might mislead a reader into thinking the $HW$ footprint is entirely removed from the decoder stage, which is usually only true if the value projections are done 'on-the-fly' for sampled points only.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'relation modeling' vs. 'deformable convolution'

comments.explanation: The authors claim deformable convolution lacks the 'element relation modeling mechanism' (the query-key interaction) and and position this as the reason for needing Deformable Transformers. However, in Section 4.1, they note that their module degenerates to deformable convolution when $L=1, K=1$, and the weight matrix is identity. This implies that the 'relation modeling' primarily comes from the inclusion of the attention weight $A_{mqk}$ (which is essentially a dynamic weight) and the Multi-Head linear projections. Since Deformable Conv v2 already introduced a modulation scalar (data-dependent weighting), the distinction between 'relation modeling' in Transformers versus 'dynamic weighting' in modern convolutions is quite thin here. A reader might find the distinction between the proposed module and a multi-head version of modulated deformable convolution to be underspecified in terms of why one constitutes 'relation modeling' and the other does not.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in attention weight distribution claim

comments.explanation: The authors are discussing why Transformers converge slowly, claiming that at initialization, attention weights are nearly uniform. However, there are two numerical issues here. First, the text says the mean of the projected features is '1 0', which is likely an OCR error for '0', as standard initializations (like Xavier or Kaiming) aim for a zero mean. Second, and more importantly, it states that $A_{mqk} \approx N_k$ when $N_k$ is large. In a softmax distribution where weights must sum to 1, a uniform distribution across $N_k$ elements should result in $A_{mqk} \approx 1/N_k$. Stating the weight is approximately $N_k$ is mathematically impossible for normalized attention and contradicts the point that the weights are 'nearly uniform' and small, leading to 'ambiguous gradients'. comments.explanation: In standard Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Vaswani et al. 2017), the dot product is scaled by $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent gradients from vanishing. Here, the OCR text shows a square root symbol over 'm' or near 'C', but the prose later defines $C_v = C/M$. Usually, the scaling factor should be $\sqrt{C_v}$ (the dimension of the head). The formula provided is quite garbled due to OCR, but it appears to place the square root in the numerator or associate it with the head index 'm' rather than the dimension. Furthermore, the transposition in $z_q^T U_m^T V_m x_k$ (implied by the symbols) usually involves a Query matrix and a Key matrix. The specific arrangement of $U$ and $V$ here, combined with the scaling factor placement, makes the derivation of the 'uniform weight' claim hard to verify formally.

comments.title: Incorrect complexity term for Standard Multi-Head Attention
comments.quote: The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of $O$ ( $NqC_[2] + NkC_[2] + NqNkC$ ).
comments.explanation: The authors break down the complexity of the standard Transformer attention. While the first two terms ($N_qC^2$ and $N_kC^2$) correctly represent the linear projections for Query, Key, and Value, the third term $N_q N_k C$ is slightly off for standard multi-head attention. The dot product of a query ($1 \times C$) and a key ($1 \times C$) takes $O(C)$ time. Doing this for all pairs results in $N_q N_k$ dot products, which is $O(N_q N_k)$. The authors include an extra factor of $C$. While the attention *aggregation* step (weight $\times$ value) does involve an $O(N_q N_k C)$ operation, the primary bottleneck often cited in literature is the $O(N^2)$ scalar matrix. If the authors intended the third term to represent the full attention mechanism including the weighted sum of values, $O(N_q N_k C)$ is correct, but usually, the 'quadratic' concern specifically refers to the $N^2$ spatial interaction. Given their argument that complexity is dominated by this term when $N \gg C$, this is likely a standard notation, but it is worth noting that the 'C' factor depends on whether one is discussing the similarity matrix or the value aggregation.

comments.title: Missing Multi-Head Index in Equation 2
comments.quote: \text{DeformAttn}(z_q, p_q, x) = \sum_{m=1}^M W_m \left[ \sum_{k=1}^K A_{mqk} W'_m x(p_q + \Delta p_{mqk}) \right]
comments.explanation: Looking at Equation 2 within the OCR-processed text, there is a mismatch regarding the projection weights $W'_m$. In the standard Multi-Head Attention definition provided in the SUMMARY section, $x_k$ is transformed by $W'_m$ inside the inner summation. However, in the expansion of Deformable Attention in Section 4.1, the prose describes $W'_m$ as having dimensions $C_v \times C$ (where $C_v = C/M$). If $W'_m$ is applied inside the inner sum over $k$, the resulting vector has dimension $C_v$. The outer sum then applies $W_m$ (dimension $C \times C_v$) to project it back to $C$. While this aligns with the MHA structure, the specific notation for the sampling points $x(p_q + \Delta p_{mqk})$ seems to be missing a corresponding head-specific feature slicing or projection index. In many implementations of Deformable Convolution or similar attention, each head $m$ operates on a specific 'slice' of the input feature $x$. If $x$ is the full feature map of dimension $C$, applying the same $W'_m$ to the full feature across all heads would be redundant and computationally expensive. Usually, $x$ would be indexed or sliced as $x_m$ to denote the $m$-th feature split. While a reader can infer this from the definition of $C_v = C/M$, the formula as written implies the full feature $x$ is used by every head, which contradicts the efficiency claims.

comments.title: Inconsistent Complexity Calculation for Deformable Attention
comments.quote: the complexity of the deformable attention module is of $O ( 2 N_q C^2 + \min( HWC^2 , N_q K C^2 ) )$
comments.explanation: The text states that the complexity of the deformable attention module is $O(2N_qC^2 + ...)$. However, the primary advantage of deformable attention is that it only samples $K$ points per query. For each query $q$ and head $m$, we perform a linear projection to get offsets and weights (3MK channels), and a final projection $W_m$. The actual feature aggregation involves $N_q \times M \times K$ sampling operations and multiplications. If $K$ is a small constant (as claimed), the complexity relative to the feature dimension $C$ should be dominated by the linear projections of the query ($N_q \times C \times 3MK \approx N_q C^2$ if $MK \approx C$) and the output projection. However, the term $\min(HWC^2, N_qKC^2)$ is confusing. In the encoder, where $N_q = HW$, this term suggests a complexity of $HWC^2$. If $K$ is a small fixed constant (e.g., 4 or 8), $N_qKC^2$ is simply $O(N_qC^2)$. The inclusion of $HWC^2$ in a $\min$ function suggests that the authors might be accounting for the cost of the initial $1 \times 1$ convolutions to produce the value features $W'_m x$, but the notation $N_qKC^2$ suggests that the cost scales with $C^2$ per sampled point, whereas sampling and weighted summation usually scale as $N_qKC$. If $C$ is the channel dimension and $x$ is already projected, the aggregation is $O(N_q K C)$. The $C^2$ term usually comes from the linear weights $W_m$ and $W'_m$. If $W'_m$ is applied to the sampled values (as in Eq 2), it is $O(N_q K C^2 / M)$. The logic is slightly circular: they claim it is linear in $HW$, but the complexity formula provided looks like a standard MHA complexity with $K$ replacing $N_k$. A clearer breakdown of the projection vs. aggregation costs would be necessary to verify the linear scaling claim fully.

comments.title: Ambiguous Reference Point Normalization in Multi-scale Equation
comments.quote: $\phi_l(\hat{p}_q)$ in Equation 3 re-scales the normalized coordinates $\hat{p}_q$ to the input feature map of the $l$-th level.
comments.explanation: Equation 3 introduces $\hat{p}_q \in [0, 1]^2$ as a normalized coordinate for multi-scale attention. It then uses $\phi_l(\hat{p}_q)$ to re-scale these to the $l$-th level feature map. However, the equation for the offsets is $\phi_l(\hat{p}_q) + \Delta p_{mlqk}$. There is a potential ambiguity here: often, sampling offsets $\Delta p$ are defined in 'pixels' relative to the resolution of the current feature map to maintain the interpretation of 'deformable' sampling. If $\Delta p$ is added to the scaled coordinates $\phi_l(\hat{p}_q)$ without itself being re-scaled or normalized to that specific level's resolution, the 'receptive field' of the offsets would change significantly across levels (e.g., an offset of 1 pixel on a high-res map represents a much smaller physical area than 1 pixel on a low-res map). The paper does not explicitly state whether the offsets are predicted in normalized units or level-specific pixel units. While functionally the network can learn to compensate, it makes the 'multi-scale' aggregation logic less intuitive for reproduction compared to standard deformable convolution where offsets are local to the grid.

comments.title: Complexity derivation inconsistency for Deformable Attention
comments.quote: when $MK$ is relatively small, the complexity of the deformable attention module is of $O (2 N_qC^2 + min( HWC^2 , N_qKC^2 ))$
comments.explanation: The text states the complexity is $O(2 N_qC^2 + \min(HWC^2, N_qKC^2))$. However, standard multi-head attention complexity usually involves terms like $N_q C^2$ for linear projections of queries to heads and $N_k C^2$ for keys. In Deformable Attention, for each query, we sample only $K$ points. The linear projection to get offsets and weights is $O(N_q C \cdot MK)$, and the subsequent aggregation is $O(N_q C \cdot K)$. The term $HWC^2$ in the $\min$ function suggests a scenario where every pixel's feature is projected once (standard convolution/attention style), while $N_qKC^2$ suggests the cost of projecting keys on-the-fly for each query. However, the Paper Summary notes 'Complexity (Deformable DETR Decoder): $O(NKC^2)$', which omits the $N_qC^2$ quadratic-in-channel overhead mentioned here. While the overarching claim of linear scaling with spatial resolution holds, the specific constants and the inclusion of $HWC^2$ within the minimum for a module that specifically 'attends to a small set... regardless of spatial size' are slightly confusing and could mislead a reader trying to calculate precise FLOPs for the encoder vs. decoder.

comments.title: Potential Indexing Ambiguity in Attention Weights
comments.quote: The scalar attention weight $A_{mqk}$ lies in the range $[0 , 1]$, normalized by $\sum_{k=1}^K A_{mqk} = 1$.
comments.explanation: In Equation 2, the sampling point index $k$ ranges from $1$ to $K$. In Equation 1 (Standard Attention), $k$ indexes the set of all keys $\Omega_k$. In the multi-scale version (Eq. 3), the normalization is $\sum_{l=1}^L \sum_{k=1}^K A_{mlqk} = 1$. The transition from $k$ being an absolute index of the key set to $k$ being a relative index of a sampling point is clear, but the authors use $k \in \Omega_k$ in Eq. 1 and then $k=1 \dots K$ in Eq. 2 without explicitly noting that $k$ in Eq. 2 no longer points to a pre-defined set of memory locations, but rather to the $k$-th predicted offset. This is mostly resolved by the description of 'sampled keys', but a reader might find the reuse of index $k$ slightly overloaded when comparing Eq. 1 and Eq. 2.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper demonstrates high quality and clarity in its exposition, successfully identifying and addressing two critical bottlenecks in the original DETR framework: slow convergence and poor performance on small objects. The introduction is particularly strong, offering a lucid analysis of why standard Transformer attention struggles with high-resolution feature maps and effectively motivating the proposed "deformable attention" as a hybrid solution that merges the spatial efficiency of deformable convolutions with the relation modeling of Transformers. comments.title: Missing equation number reference
comments.quote: The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of _O_ ( _NqC_[2] + _NkC_[2] + _NqNkC_ ).

comments.title: Potential typo in complexity analysis condition
comments.quote: when MK is relatively small, the complexity of the deformable attention module is of O(2NqC² + min(HWC², NqKC²))
comments.explanation: The text states 'when MK is relatively small', but the complexity analysis depends on K (the number of sampled keys per head), not MK (the total number of sampled keys across all heads). The term min(HWC², NqKC²) in the complexity analysis depends on K, not MK. If K is small, then NqKC² is small compared to HWC², and min(HWC², NqKC²) = NqKC². If K is large, then NqKC² could be large compared to HWC², and min(HWC², NqKC²) = HWC². So the complexity depends on K, not MK. The text should say 'when K is relatively small' instead of 'when MK is relatively small'. This is a minor typo that could be confusing for readers.

comments.title: Underspecified number of region proposals
comments.quote: Top scoring bounding boxes are picked as region proposals.
comments.explanation: The text describes the two-stage variant's proposal generation but omits the specific number of proposals selected (e.g., top-K). While the appendix is referenced for implementation details, the number of proposals is a critical hyperparameter that significantly impacts the computational cost, memory usage, and performance of the two-stage architecture. Leaving this quantity implicit in the main text makes the method description incomplete for readers seeking to understand the efficiency claims or reproduce the results without immediately consulting the appendix.

comments.title: Misattribution of Ablation Study Results in Section 5.2
comments.quote: Using multi-scale inputs instead of single-scale inputs can effectively improve detection accuracy with 1.7% AP, especially on small objects with 2.9% APS. Increasing the number of sampling points K can further improve 0.9% AP. Using multi-scale deformable attention, which allows information exchange among different scale levels, can bring additional 1.5% improvement in AP.
comments.explanation:
The text in Section 5.2 describes the performance gains of three specific architectural modifications—multi-scale inputs, increasing the number of sampling points ($K$), and using multi-scale deformable attention—but the numerical values provided are swapped and do not correspond to the correct differences shown in Table 2. 

Analyzing the progression of AP scores in Table 2 (39.7 $	o$ 41.4 $	o$ 42.3 $	o$ 43.8):
1. The increase from 39.7 to 41.4 is +1.7% AP (and +2.9% APS). This corresponds to increasing $K$ from 1 to 4 (comparing the 'degenerate' case to the single-scale, $K=4$ case). The text incorrectly attributes this gain to 'Using multi-scale inputs'.
2. The increase from 41.4 to 42.3 is +0.9% AP. This corresponds to enabling multi-scale attention. The text incorrectly attributes this gain to 'Increasing the number of sampling points K'.
3. The increase from 42.3 to 43.8 is +1.5% AP. This corresponds to adding multi-scale inputs (comparing the single-scale with multi-scale attention model to the full multi-scale model). The text incorrectly attributes this gain to 'Using multi-scale deformable attention'.

This scrambling of descriptions undermines the reader's ability to understand the contribution of each component.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound extension of DETR, named Deformable DETR, aimed at addressing two critical limitations of the original framework: slow convergence and poor performance on small objects due to the quadratic complexity of Transformer attention over high-resolution feature maps. The core idea—replacing global attention with deformable attention that samples a small, learnable set of key locations—draws effectively from prior work in deformable convolutions while retaining the global modeling capabilities of Transformers through deformable aggregations across scales. This hybrid approach strikes a compelling balance between efficiency and expressiveness. The paper is clearly written, with a logical flow from problem analysis to method design and experimental validation. The architectural diagram and integration into a multi-scale detection pipeline further enhance clarity. However, a significant concern lies in the depth of comparison with concurrent and closely related works—particularly methods like Conditional DETR and Sparse DETR—that also aim to accelerate DETR's convergence. While the related work section surveys broad attention mechanisms, it does not critically engage with these direct alternatives, potentially weakening the positioning of novelty. Additionally, the computational complexity argument, though intuitive, would benefit from more rigorous ablation (e.g., FLOPS and throughput comparisons) to substantiate claims of efficiency. Nonetheless, the 10× faster convergence and improved performance on small objects are strong empirical contributions, and the open-sourcing of code is commendable for reproducibility. Overall, the paper demonstrates high technical quality and practical impact, though a more thorough comparative discussion would strengthen its scholarly rigor.

comments.title: Ambiguous normalization in multi-scale deformable attention
comments.quote: The scalar attention weight Amlqk is normalized by[] [L] l=1  Kk=1 [A][mlqk]=[1]
comments.explanation: The normalization of attention weights in multi-scale deformable attention is ambiguously described. The text suggests a global normalization over all levels and sampling points: \sum_{l=1}^L \sum_{k=1}^K A_{mlqk} = 1 for each head m and query q. However, it is not explicitly clear whether the normalization is global, per level, or per head. This affects how attention is distributed across scales and can influence model behavior. While standard practice and likely implementation use global softmax over all L×K samples, the lack of specification impacts reproducibility. Multiple issues flagged related aspects of this (e.g., missing normalization equations, scope ambiguity), but this captures the core technical ambiguity.

comments.title: Under-specified sampling function in multi-scale attention
comments.quote: Function _l_ -th level. _φl_ (ˆThe multi-scale deformable _**p** q_ ) in Equation 3 re-scales
comments.explanation: The function \varphi_l mapping normalized reference points \hat{\mathbf{p}}_q \in [0,1]^2 to feature map coordinates at level l is not explicitly defined. While it is likely a simple spatial scaling (e.g., \varphi_l(\hat{\mathbf{p}}_q) = \hat{\mathbf{p}}_q \odot (W_l, H_l)), details about handling padding, stride, or boundary conditions are missing. This function is critical for determining sampling locations and affects implementation correctness. OCR corruption further obscures the presentation. Despite being recoverable from context, the omission impedes full reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in resolution relationship of Cl and input image
comments.quote: where _Cl_ is of resolution 2 _[l]_ lower than the input image.
comments.explanation: The statement that feature level Cl has resolution '2^l lower than the input image' is mathematically incorrect as written. Standard backbones (e.g., ResNet + FPN) produce C3, C4, C5, C6 at strides 8, 16, 32, 64 (i.e., 2^3 to 2^6), so associating level l=1 to C3 implies resolution 2^{l+2} lower, not 2^l. This misstatement could mislead readers deriving spatial dimensions or receptive fields. The intended mapping is recoverable from context (C3 to C6 over l=1 to 4), but the explicit formula is erroneous. This is not merely notational—it introduces a factual inaccuracy in a quantitative claim.

comments.title: Missing core equation for deformable attention feature aggregation
comments.quote: the deformable attention feature is calculated by
comments.explanation: The central equation defining how the deformable attention output is computed—aggregating sampled features using attention weights and interpolation—is absent from the passage. While components like offset prediction, bilinear sampling, and softmax weights are described, the synthesis into a final feature vector (e.g., \mathbf{y}_q = \sum_m \mathbf{W}_m \sum_{l,k} A_{mlqk} \cdot \mathbf{x}(\mathbf{p}_q + \Delta\mathbf{p}_{mlqk}))} is missing. This omission, whether due to truncation or oversight, renders the method underspecified in the presented text, especially given this is a key contribution. Surrounding descriptions are insufficient to reconstruct the exact aggregation mechanism without assumptions.

comments.title: Unclear justification for deformable attention efficiency claims
comments.quote: Different from Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019), deformable attention is just slightly slower than the traditional convolution under the same FLOPs.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that deformable attention is only 'slightly slower' than traditional convolution at similar FLOPs, implying strong hardware efficiency. However, no empirical benchmarks, latency measurements, or architectural rationale (e.g., memory access patterns) are provided to support this. While plausible due to shared locality with deformable convolutions, the claim is a key motivation for the method and currently rests on assertion. Related issues highlight the risk of overstating efficiency—without evidence, 'slightly slower' may misrepresent actual runtime costs, especially given known GPU inefficiencies with irregular memory access. The claim should be either supported or softened.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of scale-level embedding in encoder vs. decoder
comments.quote: To identify which feature level each query pixel lies in, we add a scale-level embedding, denoted as _**e** l_ , to the feature representation...
comments.explanation: The paper introduces trainable scale-level embeddings **e**_l in the encoder to distinguish feature pyramid levels but does not clarify their use in the decoder. Object queries in the decoder also process multi-scale features via the same attention mechanism, yet there is no mention of incorporating **e**_l into decoder queries. Given the shared multi-scale attention module, this asymmetry raises concerns about consistency. While the decoder's reference point mapping \varphi_l may implicitly encode scale, the lack of explicit discussion creates ambiguity. Is **e**_l used in both? If not, why? This underspecification risks misinterpretation of the full architecture.

comments.title: Underspecified bilinear interpolation and sampling boundaries
comments.quote: bilinear interpolation is applied as in Dai et al. (2017) in computing **x** ( **p** q +∆ **p** mqk )
comments.explanation: Although bilinear interpolation is correctly cited, the paper omits critical implementation details: how gradients are handled during backpropagation, how out-of-boundary sampling is managed (e.g., clamping, zero-padding), and whether the interpolation is differentiable at integer boundaries. Since offsets are learned and impact attention dynamically, edge behavior can affect training stability and reproducibility. While common in the literature, such omissions weaken methodological rigor—especially given the paper's focus on precise, deformable sampling.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reference point coordinates notation
comments.quote: Let **p** ˆ q ∈ [0 , 1]2 be the normalized coordinates of the reference point...
comments.explanation: The paper inconsistently uses **p**_q (without hat) and **p̂**_q (with hat) without clearly distinguishing coordinate systems. It appears that **p̂**_q denotes normalized image coordinates [0,1]², while **p**_q is in feature map or pixel coordinates. However, this distinction is not consistently maintained or formally defined. For instance, in the encoder, **p**_q is 'itself' (pixel location), but its transformation to **p̂**_q before being passed to \varphi_l is not specified. This notational ambiguity risks confusion when mapping between levels and interpolating features.
